reasoning:
Swimming world records typically last for several years, as evidenced by earlier 100m freestyle records (Cielo's lasted 13 years, Popovici's 2 years). Pan Zhanle's current record of 46.40, set at the Paris 2024 Olympics, beat the previous world record by an enormous 0.46 seconds. This represents a generational leap in the event, meaning very few (if any) active swimmers are close to breaking it. Furthermore, if Pan breaks his own record, he remains the record holder, resolving this question to YES. 

The primary risks are:
1. Someone else swimming an unthinkable sub-46.40 race.
2. Pan Zhanle having his record stripped retroactively due to a doping violation. 

While Chinese swimmers have faced intense doping scrutiny, Pan has been highly tested and was notably not implicated in the earlier mass doping case. He is currently active and just won multiple events at the Chinese National Swimming Championships in May 2025 (swimming a relaxed 47.77 in the 100m free), confirming he has no secret suspensions. 

The only major international long-course event remaining in 2025 where a rival might break his record is the World Aquatics Championships in Singapore, taking place July 27 to August 3, 2025. Therefore, the probability that the record is lost drops slightly for the August and November dates, representing the very slim chance that a competitor (like David Popovici) pulls off a miracle swim, or that a delayed doping positive emerges. For the June dates, the risk is practically zero as there are no elite international long-course championships. My probabilities reflect overwhelming confidence that Pan will retain his record, with a marginal increase in uncertainty reflecting the forecast horizon.
